---
title: "Newton-Raphson PX4 Math and Code Walkthrough"
author: "egmc"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry: margin=0.9in
execute:
  echo: false
---

# Scope

This note explains the checked-in Newton-Raphson controller implementations in
the PX4 workspace at the level of:

- the continuous-time model they use,
- the predicted-output map they invert,
- the discrete update law they apply each 100 Hz cycle,
- the difference between `baseline` and `workshop`,
- and why the method should work when its assumptions are approximately true.

The focus is the Python implementations of:

- `src/newton_raphson_px4/`
- `src/newton_raphson_enhanced_px4/`

with a short appendix for:

- `src/nr_diff_flat_px4/`

This is a code walkthrough, not a proof. The point is to make every term in the
implemented update law easy to trace back to the source.

# State, Input, and Output

The standard and enhanced Newton-Raphson controllers use the same 9-state,
4-input quadrotor model:

$$
x =
\begin{bmatrix}
p_x & p_y & p_z & v_x & v_y & v_z & \phi & \theta & \psi
\end{bmatrix}^\top
\in \mathbb{R}^9
$$

$$
u =
\begin{bmatrix}
F & p & q & r
\end{bmatrix}^\top
\in \mathbb{R}^4
$$

where:

- $F$ is total thrust in Newtons,
- $p,q,r$ are body angular rates,
- $\phi,\theta,\psi$ are roll, pitch, yaw.

The controlled output is the 4-vector

$$
y = Cx =
\begin{bmatrix}
p_x & p_y & p_z & \psi
\end{bmatrix}^\top
$$

The output-selection matrix `C` is defined directly in the controller utilities.

Code locations:

- `C` and the output definition:
  `src/newton_raphson_px4/newton_raphson_px4_utils/controller/nr_utils.py`
- same structure for the enhanced controller:
  `src/newton_raphson_enhanced_px4/newton_raphson_enhanced_px4_utils/controller/nr_utils.py`

# Continuous-Time Model Used by the Predictor

The predictor integrates the translational dynamics together with Euler-angle
kinematics.

## Angular-rate kinematics

The controller stores angular-rate commands as body rates $[p,q,r]^\top$, but
the state uses Euler angles. The helper

$$
\dot{\eta} = T(\phi,\theta)\,\omega_b
$$

maps body rates $\omega_b = [p,q,r]^\top$ into world-frame Euler angle rates
$\dot{\eta} = [\dot{\phi},\dot{\theta},\dot{\psi}]^\top$.

In code, that is `body2world_angular_rates(...)`.

## Translational dynamics

The implemented dynamics are

$$
\dot{p} = v
$$

$$
\dot{v}_x = -\frac{F}{m}\left(\sin\phi\sin\psi + \cos\phi\cos\psi\sin\theta\right)
$$

$$
\dot{v}_y = -\frac{F}{m}\left(\cos\phi\sin\psi\sin\theta - \cos\psi\sin\phi\right)
$$

$$
\dot{v}_z = g - \frac{F}{m}\cos\phi\cos\theta
$$

with $g = 9.8$ and the Euler-angle rates supplied by the $T(\phi,\theta)$ map.

So the predictor model is simply

$$
\dot{x} = f(x,u)
$$

implemented in `f_quad(...)` and wrapped by `dynamics(...)`.

Code locations:

- standard NR model:
  `src/newton_raphson_px4/newton_raphson_px4_utils/controller/nr_utils.py`
- enhanced NR model:
  `src/newton_raphson_enhanced_px4/newton_raphson_enhanced_px4_utils/controller/nr_utils.py`

# The Predicted-Output Map

At each control cycle, the Newton-Raphson controller does not invert the raw
continuous dynamics directly. Instead it inverts a *predicted output map* over a
finite lookahead horizon $T$.

Define

$$
g_T(x_k, u_{\text{prev}}, u_{\text{cand}})
=
C\,\hat{x}(t_k + T)
$$

where $\hat{x}$ is the state produced by numerically integrating the model
forward from the current state $x_k$.

The code computes this with:

1. `predict_state(...)`
2. `predict_output(...)`
3. `get_jac_pred_u(...)`
4. `get_inv_jac_pred_u(...)`

## RK4 integration

The integration method is RK4, not Euler. That matters because the controller is
using the predictor Jacobian inside the Newton step; a poor integrator would
directly degrade that Jacobian.

## ZOH versus FOH

The key prediction-model difference between `baseline` and `workshop` is how the
input is assumed to behave over the lookahead horizon:

- `baseline`: zero-order hold (ZOH), meaning the candidate control is treated as
  constant over the whole horizon.
- `workshop`: first-order hold (FOH), meaning the input is interpolated from the
  current input toward the candidate input over the horizon.

Mathematically:

### Baseline ZOH

$$
u(\tau) = u_{\text{cand}}
$$

### Workshop FOH

$$
u(\tau) =
u_{\text{prev}} +
\frac{\tau}{T}
\left(u_{\text{cand}} - u_{\text{prev}}\right)
$$

The FOH version is closer to what actually happens on a real vehicle, because a
new command does not instantaneously become the commanded operating point for the
entire future horizon.

This is implemented in `interpolate_input(...)`.

# Standard Newton-Raphson Update Law

The standard controller uses the predicted output error

$$
e_T = r_T - g_T(x_k, u_{\text{prev}}, u_{\text{cand}})
$$

where $r_T$ is the reference output evaluated at the lookahead time.

## Jacobian inversion

The controller computes

$$
J_u = \frac{\partial g_T}{\partial u_{\text{cand}}}
$$

and then uses the pseudoinverse

$$
J_u^\dagger
$$

instead of an exact matrix inverse. In practice this is a robustness choice:
even though the map is 4-by-4, local conditioning can still get ugly.

## Bounded integral action

The workshop profile adds an integral state

$$
\eta_{k+1}
=
\operatorname{sat}\left(\eta_k + e_{\text{now}}\,\Delta t\right)
$$

where:

- $e_{\text{now}}$ is the *current-time* tracking error,
- $\Delta t$ is the 100 Hz controller period,
- `sat(...)` clips each component to a configured bound.

The clipped integral is then injected into the predicted-output error:

$$
\tilde{e}_T = e_T + K_I \eta_k
$$

This is not a full integral-state augmentation of the plant. It is a pragmatic
bias-rejection term: accumulate steady offset, but clip it so it cannot wind up
without bound.

In code:

- the node accumulates and clips `nr_error_integral`,
- the controller adds `integral_gain * clipped_integral` to the predicted-output
  error before the Newton step.

## Single-step versus two-step update

The raw Newton direction is

$$
n = J_u^\dagger \tilde{e}_T
$$

The control barrier function then adds a correction $v_{\text{cbf}}$ so the
command stays within the allowed thrust and body-rate envelope.

The implemented discrete update is:

$$
u^{(i+1)}
=
u^{(i)}
+ \beta
\left[
\alpha \odot \left(n + v_{\text{cbf}}\right)
\right]\Delta t
$$

where:

- $\alpha$ is the per-channel gain vector,
- $\odot$ is elementwise multiplication,
- $\beta$ is the iteration damping,
- $i$ counts Newton refinements inside the same control cycle.

Then:

- `baseline` uses `num_iterations = 1`, `iteration_damping = 1.0`
- `workshop` uses `num_iterations = 2`, `iteration_damping = 0.65`

So the workshop version does *two smaller Newton corrections* per 100 Hz cycle
instead of one undamped correction.

## Code mapping for the standard controller

The profile definition is in:

- `src/newton_raphson_px4/newton_raphson_px4_utils/controller/newton_raphson_px4.py`

The actual implemented structure is:

$$
\tilde{e}_T
=
\underbrace{r_T - g_T(\cdot)}_{\text{predicted output error}}
+
\underbrace{K_I \eta_k}_{\text{bounded integral bias term}}
$$

$$
n = J_u^\dagger \tilde{e}_T
$$

$$
u^{(i+1)}
=
u^{(i)}
+ \beta
\left[
\alpha \odot \left(n + v_{\text{cbf}}\right)
\right]\Delta t
$$

This corresponds directly to:

- profile parameters in `build_nr_profile(...)`
- clipped integral in `clipped_integral = jnp.clip(...)`
- error injection in `error = get_tracking_error(...) + integral_gain * clipped_integral`
- Newton direction in `nr_step = dgdu_inv @ error`
- barrier correction in `get_integral_cbf(...)`
- damped update in `updated_input = candidate_input + iteration_damping * delta_u`

# Why the Standard Workshop Changes Should Help

The standard workshop profile bundles four fixes that all point in the same
direction.

## 1. Shorter lookahead

If the predictor horizon is too long, the controller is trying to invert a map
that is increasingly sensitive to model mismatch. A shorter $T$ makes
$g_T(\cdot)$ more local and therefore more believable.

## 2. FOH instead of ZOH

If the actual command will move gradually from the current input toward the next
input, then FOH is a better approximation than pretending the future input is
constant for the entire horizon.

## 3. Bounded integral action

If there is a persistent bias from mass error, unmodeled aerodynamics, or inner
loop mismatch, a pure Newton step can keep solving the wrong model and sit at a
steady offset. The bounded integral term pushes the equilibrium until that bias
is canceled, but the clipping prevents runaway windup.

## 4. Two damped Newton updates

One undamped Newton step can be too aggressive when the map is nonlinear or the
Jacobian is only locally accurate. Two damped steps are often a better trade:
more local refinement, less overshoot.

# Enhanced Newton-Raphson Update Law

The enhanced controller keeps the same predicted-output framework but adds a
reference-rate term and a state-Jacobian compensation term.

The implemented idea is:

1. still track the output error,
2. but also try to impose a desired *output-rate* dynamics.

## Desired output-rate dynamics

The enhanced law can be read as targeting

$$
\dot{y}_{\text{des}} = \dot{r} + \alpha \odot \tilde{e}_T
$$

That is: follow the reference rate $\dot{r}$ while still correcting output error
through the proportional term.

## First-order output dynamics approximation

Using the chain rule,

$$
\dot{y}
\approx
\frac{\partial g_T}{\partial x} f(x,u)
+
\frac{\partial g_T}{\partial u}\,\dot{u}
$$

Define:

$$
J_x = \frac{\partial g_T}{\partial x}
,\qquad
J_u = \frac{\partial g_T}{\partial u}
$$

Then solve for $\dot{u}$:

$$
\dot{u}
=
J_u^\dagger
\left(
\dot{r}
- J_x f(x,u)
+ \alpha \odot \tilde{e}_T
\right)
$$

This is exactly the meaning of the enhanced correction term

$$
\dot{r} - J_x f(x,u)
$$

that appears in the implementation.

## Implemented enhanced update

The enhanced code computes:

$$
\text{regular\_term} = \alpha \odot \tilde{e}_T
$$

$$
\text{enhanced\_term} = \dot{r} - J_x f(x,u)
$$

$$
n = J_u^\dagger \left(\text{regular\_term} + \text{enhanced\_term}\right)
$$

and then uses the same barrier-corrected, damped discrete update:

$$
u^{(i+1)}
=
u^{(i)}
+ \beta (n + v_{\text{cbf}})\Delta t
$$

with `baseline` and `workshop` changing the same profile knobs as in standard NR:

- lookahead,
- integral term,
- number of iterations,
- damping,
- FOH versus ZOH.

Code locations:

- `get_jac_pred_x_uinv(...)`
- `get_enhanced_error(...)`
- `newton_raphson_enhanced(...)`

# Why the Enhanced Controller Is More Finicky

This workspace’s results are consistent with the structure of the enhanced law:
it can be better, but it is also easier to destabilize.

Why:

## 1. It needs more model accuracy

Standard NR mostly asks the predictor to map command changes to future output
changes. Enhanced NR also asks for a good estimate of

$$
J_x f(x,u)
$$

which increases sensitivity to model mismatch.

## 2. It needs good reference-rate information

The term $\dot{r}$ is not free. If the timing, interpolation, or trajectory
derivative is noisy or inconsistent with the actual flown path, the enhanced law
inherits that error directly.

## 3. It is effectively more aggressive

Even if `alpha` is numerically unchanged, the enhanced law is not “just the same
controller plus a small tweak.” It adds another structured term inside the
Jacobian inversion. That can help on some trajectories and hurt on others.

So the enhanced controller *should* work best when:

- the local predicted-output model is accurate,
- the Jacobian is well-conditioned,
- the reference derivatives are trustworthy,
- and the trajectory is smooth enough that the extra first-order correction term
  is actually informative.

# How the Node Wires the Math Into a 100 Hz Loop

In the runtime node, each control cycle does roughly this:

1. Compute the lookahead reference time:

   $$
   t_{\text{ref}} = t_{\text{traj}} + T_{\text{lookahead}}
   $$

2. Evaluate:

   - the future reference $r_T$,
   - the future reference derivative $\dot{r}_T$ when needed,
   - and the current-time reference $r_{\text{now}}$ for the integral term.

3. Update the bounded integral state:

   $$
   \eta_{k+1}
   =
   \operatorname{sat}(\eta_k + e_{\text{now}}\Delta t)
   $$

4. Call the JAX controller with:

   - current state,
   - previous input,
   - profile parameters,
   - current integral state,
   - and reference data.

5. Convert the returned thrust force into a PX4 throttle-equivalent command and
   publish rates setpoints.

The standard node code is a good place to read the full path because it is the
least complicated variant.

# Profile Tables

## Standard NR

| Profile | Lookahead | Predictor | Iterations | `alpha` | Integral |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `baseline` | `1.2 s` | ZOH | `1` | `[50, 60, 60, 60]` | disabled |
| `workshop` | `0.8 s` | FOH | `2` | `[45, 55, 55, 45]` | bounded, enabled |

## Enhanced NR

| Profile | Lookahead | Predictor | Iterations | `alpha` | Integral |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `baseline` | `1.2 s` | ZOH | `1` | `[30, 40, 40, 40]` | disabled |
| `workshop` | `0.8 s` | FOH | `2` | `[30, 40, 40, 40]` | bounded, enabled |

# Appendix: Diff-Flat NR Uses the Same Workshop Pattern

The differential-flatness controller is not built from the same $g_T(x,u)$ map,
but its `baseline` versus `workshop` split follows the same structural logic.

Its checked-in profile changes are:

- shorter lookahead (`0.8 s -> 0.5 s`)
- bounded integral term
- two damped iterations (`1 -> 2`, damping `1.0 -> 0.65`)
- slightly higher `alpha`

The diff-flat controller’s inner update is:

$$
z_{3}^{(i+1)}
=
z_{3}^{(i)}
+ \beta\,u_{\text{df,corrected}}\,\Delta t
$$

where the workshop variant again uses integral error injection and two damped
iterations inside the same 100 Hz cycle.

So conceptually, the whole workspace is using the same workshop idea in three
different ways:

- standard NR: better predicted-output inversion,
- enhanced NR: same, plus output-rate compensation,
- diff-flat NR: same, but in flat-output coordinates.

# Practical Reading Order

If you want to understand the implementation efficiently, read in this order:

1. `docs/qmd/newton_raphson_workshop_profiles.qmd`
2. `src/newton_raphson_px4/newton_raphson_px4_utils/controller/nr_utils.py`
3. `src/newton_raphson_px4/newton_raphson_px4_utils/controller/newton_raphson_px4.py`
4. `src/newton_raphson_px4/newton_raphson_px4/ros2px4_node.py`
5. `src/newton_raphson_enhanced_px4/newton_raphson_enhanced_px4_utils/controller/nr_enhanced.py`

That progression matches the logical order:

- model,
- predictor,
- Newton update,
- node wiring,
- enhanced correction term.
